title: The one time you actually need Enter
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the listing instead of fixing the address. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed:
A tag is open and never closed. (
does not count — it has no closing twin.)
hint2.not-closed: Do not look for </br>. There is no such tag.
hint3.not-closed:
Check the

and

tags. There should be two paragraphs, each one closed.
why.split-into-p:
The address is not one paragraph. Break it into separate

tags and the lines fly apart, with gaps between them, like three separate thoughts. But they are not three thoughts — they are one address.
hint2.split-into-p:
Count the

tags in your code. There should be exactly two: the price and the address.
hint3.split-into-p: The address is not a list either — you cannot reorder the street and the city. It is one thought, written across several lines.
hint4.split-into-p:
Keep the address in ONE

. You say the new lines inside it, not with new paragraphs.
why.no-br:
The address is still glued together. Enter does not work — you know that by now. You have to SAY that the line ends here:
. There are four lines, so there are three breaks.
hint2.no-br: Count where you want a line to end. After "Pickup address:", after the street, after the floor. Three places.
hint3.no-br:
Ivan
Petrov

— one paragraph, two lines. The tag sits BETWEEN them, not at the start.
hint4.no-br:
Put a
at the end of every line except the last. Not after Dobrich — nothing follows it.
why.br-for-air:
You stacked
next to each other. That is exactly what you must not do.
does not mean "gap", it means "the line ends here, but the thought does not". Gaps are a job for CSS.
hint2.br-for-air:
Two
in a row describe nothing. They just push the text down.
hint3.br-for-air: It looks right, and that is exactly why it is a trap. Tomorrow, when you add CSS, those empty lines will get in your way — and you will not remember why they are there.
hint4.br-for-air:
One
per break. Never two glued together.
why.lost-address: Part of the address is gone. The task is to lay it out on lines, not to shorten it.
hint2.lost-address: All four lines must be there: Pickup address:, the street, the floor, the city.
blocks.0.text: You added a pickup address. You wrote it on three lines, the way an address is written. Run it.
blocks.1.text:
It is glued into one line. You already know this — the browser ignores your Enter. And you know the fix: wrap each line in a

.
blocks.2.text: Except it is not a fix. Try it. The three lines fly apart, with gaps between them, like three separate thoughts. But they are not three thoughts — they are one address. And they are not a list either: you cannot reorder the street and the city.
blocks.3.text:
One paragraph. A new line inside it. That is exactly what
is for.
blocks.7.text:
has no
. There is nothing for it to wrap — it holds no text, it just does something on the spot. There are only a few tags like this and you will meet them:
,
, and later and .
blocks.9.text:
Right about now you will want to stack two or three
in a row to space something out. Do not.
does not mean "gap", it means "the line ends here, but the thought does not". Gaps are a job for CSS. Every
you add for air will get in your way tomorrow.
blocks.10.text: An address, a verse, a line of a song — yes. Air between paragraphs — no.
blocks.12.text: Its relative. It shows up as a horizontal line, but it does not mean "line" — it means the topic changes here. Between the listing and the contacts below it, for example. If you just want a decorative line, again: CSS.
blocks.13.text: Your turn. Make the address sit on separate lines while staying a single paragraph.
